The default page size is 25; the maximum is 200. Cursor values are valid for 24 hours; requests with an expired cursor receive HTTP 400 with a cursor_expired type. Offset-based pagination is not supported, as it produces inconsistent results on frequently changing datasets. SDKs and Client Libraries Official SDKs are maintained by Apex and published under the Apache-2.0 license. Available for Python 3.10 and later, TypeScript 5.0 and later, Java 17 LTS and later, and Go 1.22 and later. Each SDK provides typed models for all request and response objects, automatic retry logic with exponential backoff, transparent token refresh, rate-limit handling, and streaming support for SSE query results. SDK releases are versioned independently from the API. Each SDK documents which API versions it supports. Customers using a supported SDK are notified of API deprecations through the SDK's release notes. Community-maintained SDKs for Ruby and .NET are linked from the developer portal but are not supported by Apex. SDK installation examples: Python: pip install apex-sdk. TypeScript: npm install @apex-systems/sdk. Go: go get github.com/apex-systems/apex-go. Support and SLA API support is available through the in-product support widget, the customer portal, and a dedicated Slack Connect channel for Enterprise customers. Response time SLAs by severity: P1 (service unavailable) — 15 minutes to engagement, 4 hours to resolution target. P2 (degraded service) — 1 hour to engagement. P3 (questions or feature requests) — 1 business day. The API health status, including current incidents and historical uptime, is published at status.apex-systems.example. Customers should subscribe to status page updates to receive notifications when incidents are declared and resolved.

The quarterly package includes the income statement and balance sheet with prior-quarter and prior-year comparisons, a cash flow statement, a debt covenant compliance certificate, and a variance narrative prepared by FP&A. Material subsequent events — transactions or developments that occur between the balance sheet date and the financial statement issuance date and that are significant to users — are disclosed in a subsequent events note. Apex Systems Platform Architecture Revision 4.2 — January 2026 This document is the authoritative reference for the Apex Systems production architecture. It is maintained by the Platform Engineering team, reviewed quarterly, and updated after any change that alters a named component, a communication boundary, or a stated reliability target. Engineers should not rely on tribal knowledge; if something contradicts this document, raise it in the #platform-eng Slack channel and file a correction ticket. Overview The Apex platform is a multi-tenant SaaS product that ingests, transforms, and serves analytical data for enterprise customers in financial services, healthcare, and the public sector. Customers use Apex to centralize data from disparate source systems, apply compliance-aware transformations, and expose clean, governed datasets to analysts, dashboards, and downstream APIs. The system is organized into four independently operated planes: ingestion, storage, query, and control. Each plane is a set of independently releasable services with its own release cadence, on-call rotation, and reliability budget. Planes communicate exclusively through documented APIs and asynchronous event streams. No direct database access across plane boundaries is permitted. Runtime and Deployment Infrastructure All services run on Kubernetes clusters hosted in Amazon Web Services across three regions. The primary region is us-east-1 and serves the majority of customer traffic. The warm standby is us-west-2, which maintains a synchronized replica of the storage and control planes and can absorb full traffic within 10 minutes of a primary region failure. The third region, eu-central-1, serves European customers subject to GDPR data residency requirements; data written in eu-central-1 never leaves the region under any circumstances, including disaster recovery. Each region runs an EKS cluster with three dedicated worker node groups. General-purpose nodes use m6i.large instances and host control plane services, ingestion APIs, and background workers that are not CPU or memory bound. Memory-optimized nodes use r6i.xlarge instances and host the Trino query engine, which requires large JVM heap sizes proportional to query complexity and concurrent user count. Compute-optimized nodes use c6i.2xlarge instances and host the Rust-based ingestion workers, which benefit from high core counts for parallel parsing. Autoscaling is managed by Karpenter, which provisions and terminates nodes within approximately 30 seconds of a scaling signal. The target utilization is 60% CPU across the node group, providing headroom for burst traffic. Pod autoscaling uses the Kubernetes HPA for CPU-bound services and KEDA for event-driven scaling of Kafka consumers. Ingestion Plane Customer data enters the platform through the ingestion API, a horizontally scaled FastAPI service running on general-purpose nodes. The API accepts two modes of input. Batch uploads accept files up to 5 GB delivered as a multipart HTTP POST; the file is written directly to a per-tenant staging bucket in S3 with server-side encryption using a customer-managed KMS key, and a processing job is enqueued. Streaming events are delivered through a webhook endpoint that writes messages to a per-tenant Kafka partition in near real time. The ingestion workers, written in Rust, consume from Kafka topics and process records in parallel. Each worker validates records against the tenant-defined schema stored in the schema registry, applies PII detection rules configured per

Apex Systems Budget Policy Fiscal Year 2026 — Effective January 1, 2026 This policy defines how annual and quarterly budgets are constructed, approved, managed, and monitored at Apex Systems. It applies to every cost center owner, functional leader, finance business partner, and any employee with purchase authority. Understanding this policy is a prerequisite for any employee who manages a budget line, approves vendor contracts, or initiates headcount requests. Budget Cycle and Calendar The fiscal year at Apex runs from January 1 through December 31. Budget planning follows a structured annual calendar designed to give functional leaders enough time to build thoughtful plans while giving finance enough time to consolidate, stress-test, and present a credible plan to the board. September 1: The CFO issues top-down guidance to each functional leader. The guidance specifies the target revenue growth rate for the year, the total headcount budget by function, and the maximum operating expense envelope. It is not a blank check for functional leaders to fill — it is a constraint to plan within. September 1 to October 1: Functional leaders conduct internal planning sessions. Each leader works with their finance business partner to identify the investments required to meet the revenue and efficiency targets. Decisions made during this window should be grounded in concrete business cases, not aspirational wish lists. October 15: Functional leaders submit their bottoms-up plans to the finance business partner. Plans are submitted in the standardized budget template, which includes: personnel cost by level and role, software and tools cost by vendor, professional services, facilities and travel, and a narrative memo explaining how the investments connect to company objectives. October 15 to November 15: Finance consolidates the submissions, identifies overlaps and gaps, runs sensitivity analyses, and produces the integrated company plan. Finance schedules individual working sessions with each functional leader to reconcile where the bottoms-up plan exceeds the top-down envelope. November 15 to December 5: The senior leadership team reviews the integrated plan in two sessions, one focused on revenue and go-to-market, one focused on operating expense and headcount. Material trade-offs are surfaced for CEO-level decision. December board meeting: The board of directors reviews and approves the final annual plan, including the total headcount target, the revenue plan, the operating expense budget, and the capital expenditure budget. December 20: Functional leaders receive their approved cost center budgets and are expected to communicate the plan to their teams before January 1. Mid-Year Reforecasts In addition to the annual plan, Apex produces two formal reforecasts per year. Q2 reforecast (June): Based on actual results through May and a revised assessment of second-half performance, the Q2 reforecast updates the full-year outlook for revenue, operating expense, headcount additions, and ending cash balance. The Q2 reforecast may result in approved budget adjustments for functions that are tracking significantly ahead of or behind plan. Q3 reforecast (September): The Q3 reforecast is the last opportunity to revise the current-year outlook before the annual planning cycle begins again. It also serves as a first look at the following year, providing early signals about where investment levels should increase or

Rental cars: the standard rental class is intermediate or equivalent. SUV, luxury, and sports classes require pre-approval from the manager. Rental cars should be used only when public transit or rideshare is not available or practical. The Apex corporate card provides secondary insurance coverage; primary collision coverage can be declined at the rental counter unless the rental is in a country where the card coverage does not apply. Personal vehicle: mileage for business use of a personal vehicle is reimbursed at the IRS standard mileage rate, which is updated in the Navan profile at the beginning of each year. Commuting between home and a fixed work location is not reimbursable. Parking at the employee's regular office is covered by the commuter benefit, not expense reimbursement. Non-Reimbursable Expenses The following expenses are explicitly excluded from reimbursement regardless of business context: personal clothing or personal care items, traffic violations and parking fines, airline or hotel loyalty status-optimization routing (e.g., deliberately taking a longer route to earn miles), purchase of travel or entertainment for spouses, partners, or family members except at company-sponsored events, personal entertainment subscriptions, home internet or phone upgrades beyond business use, and expenses incurred while on personal vacation even if travel is combined with a business trip. For combined personal and business trips, only the incremental cost of the business portion is reimbursable. For example, if an employee is already traveling to a personal vacation destination and adds a customer meeting, only the transportation to the customer meeting from the vacation location is reimbursable, not the cost of the trip itself. Corporate Card Program Employees in roles that involve travel more than twice per quarter are eligible for an Apex corporate card issued through Navan. Cards are Mastercard corporate liability cards; charges are billed to Apex, not the employee. Corporate cardholders must submit receipts in Navan within 7 days of each transaction. Statements reconcile at month end; unreconciled transactions are escalated to the cardholder's manager. Corporate cards may not be used for personal purchases. Charges that cannot be supported with a business receipt are the personal liability of the cardholder and are deducted from the employee's paycheck with written notice. Cards are suspended automatically for repeated late reconciliation. Card privileges are revoked on the last day of employment. Exceptions and Dispute Resolution Requests for exceptions to these guidelines must be submitted in Navan as a pre-approval request, ideally before the expense is incurred. The finance business partner reviews exception requests within 3 business days. Exceptions that are outside the finance business partner's authority are escalated to the VP of Finance. Denied expense reimbursements may be appealed in writing to the VP of Finance within 10 business days of the denial. The appeal must include the original expense report, the denial reason, and the employee's explanation of why the expense should be reimbursed. The VP of Finance's decision is final. Budget Structure and Coding Budgets are organized by cost center. Each cost center is a six-digit code assigned by Finance and corresponds to a single functional team with one designated owner and one finance business partner. Cost center owners are responsible for all spending within their center. Expenses are classified by natural account, which identifies the type of expense: 6100 — Personnel (salaries, bonus, benefits, payroll taxes) 6200 — Software and SaaS subscriptions 6300 — Professional services and consulting 6400 — Facilities and utilities 6500 — Marketing and demand generation 6600 — Travel and entertainment 6700 — Other operating expenses Every expense transaction committed in the accounting system must carry a cost center code, a natural account code, and, for project-tracked spend, a project code assigned by Finance. Transactions missing required codes are returned to the submitter; vendors are not paid until coding is complete. Capitalized Versus Operating Expense Classification Software developed for internal use must be assessed for capitalization eligibility under ASC 350-40. A project is eligible for capitalization when three conditions are met: the project has entered the application development stage (preliminary project activities, including evaluation and selection of alternatives, are complete), the project has a defined scope and estimated budget, and management has authorized and committed resources to completing the project. Costs eligible for capitalization include external consulting fees for development work, employee salaries for time directly attributable to development, and interest during development. Costs that are not eligible include training, data migration, maintenance, and overhead allocations. The Finance team maintains a capitalization register for all active projects. Cost center owners must notify Finance when a project begins the application development stage, so that capitalization can begin from the correct date. The capitalization register is reviewed quarterly; projects that are no longer in active development are closed and no further costs are capitalized. Cloud infrastructure costs, third-party SaaS subscriptions regardless of contract length, and routine maintenance are expensed as incurred. Prepaid software licenses greater than 12 months are recognized on a straight-line amortization schedule. Apex Systems Employee Handbook Effective January 1, 2026 Welcome to Apex Systems. This handbook summarizes the policies, expectations, and benefits that apply to every full-time and part-time employee. It is not a contract of employment, and the company reserves the right to revise any policy at any time with reasonable notice. Employees receive written notice of material changes through the HR portal at least 14 days before the change takes effect. By continuing employment after notice of a change, employees acknowledge the updated policy. Mission and Values Apex Systems builds enterprise data infrastructure for regulated industries including financial services, healthcare, insurance, and the public sector. Our four core values define how we work and how we make decisions. Customer obsession means starting every conversation, design review, and postmortem with the customer experience. When a product decision has an ambiguous right answer, the team defaults to the option that reduces friction for the customer. Customer feedback is surfaced in weekly product reviews and acts as direct input into quarterly roadmap planning. Technical rigor means shipping code that is correct, observable, and maintainable. Engineers are expected to review each other's designs before implementation, write tests at every layer, and participate in on-call rotations for services they own. Cutting corners to hit a deadline is treated as a short-term decision with long-term costs that must be consciously acknowledged, documented, and tracked. Candor means sharing honest perspectives even when they are unpopular. Employees are expected to disagree and commit, speak up in meetings rather than relitigating decisions in private channels, and deliver feedback directly to the person who needs to hear it. Leaders are held to a higher standard of candor because they set the tone. Ownership means finishing what you started, following up without being asked, and treating company resources as if they were your own. Every employee has the authority to raise an issue and propose a solution regardless of title or tenure. Work Hours and Location Core collaboration hours are 10:00 to 16:00 in each employee's local time zone. These hours represent the window during which employees are expected to be available for meetings, reviews, and urgent conversations. Outside of core hours, employees may structure their time around personal obligations as long as customer commitments, on-call responsibilities, and deadlines are met. Apex operates on a hybrid schedule. Engineers, designers, and product managers are expected in the office on Tuesday and Thursday each week. Sales, customer success, and go-to-market roles are expected in the office Monday through Thursday. Employees who live more than 50 miles from the nearest Apex office are classified as fully remote and are not subject to the in-office requirement. Fully remote arrangements for roles that carry an office requirement must be requested in writing and require VP approval and a documented business case. Requests are reviewed within 10 business days. Approved remote arrangements are revisited annually. Apex Systems Hiring Process Effective January 1, 2026 This document describes the end-to-end hiring process at Apex Systems, from the decision to open a new role through the candidate's first day of employment. It applies to all hiring managers, the People Operations team, recruiters, and any employee who participates in interviews or hiring decisions. The goal of this process is to identify, assess, and hire candidates who are technically qualified, aligned with Apex Systems values, and positioned to contribute meaningfully to their team from day one. Headcount Planning and Role Authorization All new hire requests must originate from an approved headcount plan. Headcount plans are submitted by department leaders during the annual budgeting process each November and approved by the Chief Executive Officer and Chief Financial Officer before the start of the new fiscal year. Mid-cycle additions to approved headcount require a written business case submitted to the department head and CFO. The business case must include the role description, expected start date, compensation band, and the business impact of leaving the position unfilled. Once headcount is authorized, the hiring manager submits a Job Requisition Form to People Operations. The form captures the job title, reporting relationship, level, compensation band, preferred start date, target candidate profile, and any specific technical requirements. People Operations will acknowledge the requisition within two business days and assign a recruiter. Job Description Development The recruiter works with the hiring manager to develop a job description within five business days of requisition acknowledgment. Job descriptions must include the role title, department, reporting structure, a summary of the role, a list of core responsibilities, required qualifications, preferred qualifications, and the compensation range. Apex Systems posts salary ranges on all external job postings in compliance with applicable pay transparency laws. Job descriptions are reviewed by the hiring manager, the department head, and People Operations before posting. Engineering roles additionally require review by the relevant technical lead to confirm accuracy of technical requirements. People Operations maintains a library of base job description templates that may be adapted for each requisition. Job Posting and Candidate Sourcing Approved job descriptions are posted on the Apex Systems careers page, LinkedIn, and any relevant industry-specific job boards selected by the recruiter. Positions are posted for a minimum of ten business days before the recruiter presents a shortlist to the hiring manager, unless the hiring manager requests a faster timeline due to business urgency. Internal candidates are encouraged to apply. Any employee who has been in their current role for at least six months and has received a satisfactory performance rating in their most recent review is eligible to apply for open positions. Internal applicants are notified of their status within five business days of application. Referrals from current employees are tracked through the applicant tracking system, and referring employees are eligible for the referral bonus outlined in the compensation guide once the referred candidate completes ninety days of employment. Passive candidate sourcing is also permitted. Recruiters and hiring managers may reach out directly to candidates through LinkedIn and professional networks. All candidate communications must be

in their first month. Onboarding Timeline Day one through day five is orientation week. New hires attend a structured orientation program run by People Operations that covers Apex Systems mission, values, organizational structure, benefits enrollment, security policies, and required compliance training. New hires meet their manager, direct teammates, and key cross-functional partners during week one. Days six through thirty focus on role ramp-up. The new hire completes role-specific training, reviews key documents and processes, shadows team members, and begins contributing to lower-complexity work. The hiring manager schedules weekly one-on-ones during the first ninety days to provide feedback and support. Days thirty-one through sixty focus on independent contribution. The new hire takes ownership of defined workstreams or responsibilities with support available as needed. By the end of sixty days, the new hire should have a clear understanding of team priorities, key processes, and stakeholder relationships. Days sixty-one through ninety focus on full contribution. The new hire is expected to be operating independently within their defined scope. At the ninety-day mark, the manager conducts an informal check-in to assess progress, discuss any challenges, and confirm alignment on goals for the remainder of the performance year. Equal Opportunity and Non-Discrimination Apex Systems is an equal opportunity employer. All hiring decisions are made without regard to race, color, religion, sex, national origin, age, disability, genetic information, veteran status, sexual orientation, gender identity, or any other characteristic protected by applicable federal, state, or local law. Interviewers who observe conduct during the hiring process that may constitute discrimination should report it to People Operations immediately. Interview panelists are required to complete unconscious bias training before participating in hiring. The training is available through the learning management system and must be renewed annually. Record Retention Hiring documentation including job postings, applications, interview notes, evaluation forms, offer letters, and background check results must be retained for a minimum of three years after the position is filled or closed. Documentation is stored in the applicant tracking system and HRIS. Physical documents, if any, are stored in locked files maintained by People Operations.

The template requires: a concise incident summary, an impact statement with quantified customer and business effect, a detailed timeline, a root cause analysis, a list of contributing factors, and a set of action items with named owners and target dates. Action items are created as Jira tickets with the label postmortem-action. They appear in the monthly reliability review dashboard. Action items that are not resolved within 30 days are escalated to the engineering manager for the responsible team. Change Freeze Apex observes a mandatory change freeze from December 20 through January 2 each year to protect customers during the end-of-year period. Additionally, change freezes may be declared by the VP of Engineering for periods surrounding major customer events such as contract renewals, audit windows, or penetration tests. During a change freeze, only rollbacks and security-critical changes may be deployed. Security-critical changes are those that address a confirmed actively exploited vulnerability or correct a data exposure. Each such deployment requires written approval from the VP of Engineering, documented in the exceptions log with a severity justification and a description of the change.

Recurring journal entries are documented in a recurring entry schedule and approved quarterly. Reconciliation controls: every balance sheet account is reconciled monthly to a subledger or external source. Reconciliations are signed off by the preparer and a second reviewer. Unreconciled items over $5,000 that are open for more than 30 days are escalated to the Controller. Access controls: access to financial systems, including the ERP, the billing system, and the equity management platform, is provisioned by the IT team based on role-based access control policies approved by Finance. Access is reviewed quarterly; access that is no longer appropriate for the current role is revoked within 5 business days of identification. Tax and Regulatory Compliance The Tax team, in coordination with external tax advisors, manages all tax compliance obligations across federal, state, local, and international jurisdictions. Federal income tax: Apex files a federal corporate income tax return annually. The federal return is prepared by an external Big Four accounting firm and reviewed by internal tax counsel before filing. Quarterly estimated tax payments are made if the company is profitable; the payment schedule is set by the external advisor. State and local income tax: Apex files income tax returns in every state where it has nexus, defined as either a physical presence (office or employees) or economic nexus based on revenue thresholds. The nexus study is updated annually. State tax payments are timed to avoid underpayment penalties. Sales and use tax: Apex's SaaS revenue is subject to sales tax in approximately 30 U.S. states with varying rules for software as a service. Avalara administers sales tax calculation, collection, and filing on behalf of Apex. Any customer that claims a tax exemption must provide a valid exemption certificate, which is archived in Avalara. International: Apex has legal entities in the United Kingdom, Canada, and Germany. Each entity files statutory financial statements and tax returns in its local jurisdiction, prepared by local advisors and consolidated into the parent company accounts. Transfer pricing: intercompany transactions between the U.S. parent and international subsidiaries are governed by a transfer pricing policy prepared by a specialized advisor and documented with a country-by-country report. The policy is reviewed and updated annually or when the intercompany transaction profile changes materially. Document Retention All financial records — general ledger entries, journal entry support, contracts, invoices, payroll records, tax filings, audit workpapers, and board materials — are retained for a minimum of seven years in the document management system. Tax records that relate to ongoing disputes or uncertain tax positions are retained until the matter is resolved plus the applicable statute of limitations. Records are stored with immutable audit trails, role-based access, and encryption at rest. Destruction of financial records before the retention period expires requires General Counsel approval. Physical documents, where applicable, are digitized and destroyed using a certified secure shredding service.

Approved remote arrangements are revisited annually. Onboarding New hires complete a structured 30-60-90 day plan developed by HR in collaboration with the hiring manager before the employee's start date. The plan is shared with the new hire on day one and updated collaboratively throughout the first quarter. During week one, new hires complete security awareness training, data handling compliance modules, and role-specific access provisioning. They are introduced to their buddy — a volunteer colleague outside of the immediate team — who provides an informal guide to working at Apex. By day 30, each new hire should have a working understanding of the product, have met every member of their immediate team, and have shipped one measurable deliverable, however small. By day 60, they should be operating independently on their core responsibilities with periodic check-ins. By day 90, they should be contributing at the level expected for their role and level. HR schedules a formal 90-day check-in with the HR Business Partner for the department. The check-in covers performance against the 30-60-90 plan, integration into the team, and any concerns the employee or manager has not yet raised formally. Feedback from new hires is aggregated quarterly to identify onboarding process improvements. Performance Reviews Formal reviews occur twice per year: a mid-year review in March and an annual review in September. The September review is the primary cycle for compensation decisions and promotion approvals. The March review is developmental; it does not carry compensation changes but may trigger an early promotion if the case is exceptional. The review process begins three weeks before the review meeting. Employees complete a self-evaluation covering accomplishments against their goals, demonstration of core values, and growth opportunities. Managers solicit feedback from four to eight peer reviewers and one or two cross-functional partners identified by the employee and the manager jointly. Peer feedback is collected through the Lattice platform and is confidential to the manager; summaries, not verbatim quotes, are shared in the review. Managers write a calibrated narrative that incorporates self-evaluation, peer feedback, and the manager's direct observations. The narrative is reviewed by the manager's manager and, for Director and above roles, by the HR Business Partner before delivery. Calibration sessions across teams at the same level ensure ratings are consistent. Ratings follow a five-point scale: exceptional, exceeds expectations, meets expectations, developing, and unsatisfactory. The expected distribution for a healthy, well-hired team is roughly 10% exceptional, 25% exceeds expectations, 55% meets expectations, 8% developing, and 2% unsatisfactory. Managers whose teams deviate significantly from this distribution are asked to explain the deviation at calibration. Performance improvement plans are initiated by HR when an employee receives an unsatisfactory rating or fails to improve from a developing rating after two consecutive review cycles. A PIP specifies measurable goals, a support plan, and a timeline of 60 to 90 days. Completion of a PIP does not guarantee continued employment but does demonstrate good-faith commitment from both parties. Professional Development

throughout the subscription period, subscription revenue is recognized on a straight-line basis from the date access is granted through the contract end date. Annual subscriptions are typically billed upfront, creating deferred revenue that is recognized ratably over 12 months. Professional services revenue represents implementation, configuration, and training services contracted separately from the platform subscription. Revenue is recognized as services are delivered using an input-based measure of progress, measured by hours incurred relative to total estimated hours. Significant deviations between estimated and actual hours are investigated by the project manager and revenue operations to determine whether the estimate should be updated. Usage-based revenue above a committed minimum is recognized in the period consumed. Metering data is extracted from the platform at month end, validated against the billing system, and recorded in the revenue subledger before close. Usage that exceeds the committed contractual minimum triggers an overage invoice within 15 days of month end. Contract modifications are evaluated to determine whether they constitute a termination of the existing contract combined with a new contract, a prospective change, or a cumulative catch-up adjustment. The Revenue Operations team consults with Finance on each modification that changes the total transaction price by more than $50,000. Deferred Revenue and Billings The deferred revenue balance represents amounts invoiced to customers for services not yet delivered. Deferred revenue is the single largest liability on the Apex balance sheet and is reviewed in detail at each quarter end by the external auditors. The deferred revenue subledger tracks each contract individually, with the original contract start date, the contract end date, the total transaction price, the cumulative amount recognized, and the remaining deferred balance. The subledger is reconciled to the general ledger daily; any reconciling item is investigated and resolved before the next business day. Billings — the total value of invoices issued in a period — is a key operating metric tracked separately from recognized revenue. Billings growth reflects new sales momentum and renewal health. The difference between billings and revenue in any period represents the change in deferred revenue. FP&A monitors the billings-to-revenue ratio monthly as a leading indicator of future revenue growth. Key Performance Indicators and Metrics Apex tracks both GAAP financial metrics and non-GAAP operating metrics. All metric definitions are documented in the metrics dictionary maintained by FP&A. Changes to any definition require CFO approval and must be disclosed in reporting materials with a note identifying the period in which the change took effect and the impact of the change on prior periods. GAAP metrics: total revenue, gross profit, gross margin percentage, operating expense by natural account, operating income (loss), net income (loss), and cash and cash equivalents. Non-GAAP operating metrics: annual recurring revenue (ARR), defined as the annualized value of all active subscription contracts as of the measurement date, excluding one-time fees and services. Net new ARR, the change in ARR from one period to the next. Gross dollar retention (GDR), the percentage of beginning ARR retained from existing customers excluding expansions, measured annually. Parental Leave Apex provides 16 weeks of fully paid parental leave for the primary caregiver and 8 weeks of fully paid parental leave for the secondary caregiver. These benefits apply to the birth, adoption, or foster placement of a child and to both biological and adoptive parents. Leave may be taken in a single continuous block or split into a maximum of two separate blocks within 12 months of the qualifying event. Employees must notify their manager and HR at least 30 days before the intended start date when the timing is foreseeable. For birth events, notification at the time of awareness is expected; the 30-day requirement is waived if the birth occurs earlier than anticipated. During parental leave, Apex continues all benefits at the normal employee premium contribution rate. Parental leave runs concurrently with any federal Family and Medical Leave Act entitlements. Employees who have worked at Apex for fewer than 12 months or fewer than 1,250 hours in the preceding 12 months are not eligible for FMLA protection but remain eligible for paid parental leave. The returning employee has the right to the same position or an equivalent position with the same base pay, bonus opportunity, and equity. Apex does not permit role changes, team transfers, or reporting structure changes initiated by the company during an employee's parental leave without the employee's written consent. Managers may not contact employees on parental leave for work-related matters except in documented emergencies with HR Business Partner approval. Employees who voluntarily maintain contact with the team during leave do so at their own discretion. Bereavement Leave Employees receive up to 5 paid days of bereavement leave for the loss of a spouse, domestic partner, child, parent, sibling, or grandparent. Employees receive up to 3 paid days for the loss of an in-law, step-parent, step-sibling, or step-child. Employees receive up to 1 paid day for the loss of a close friend or colleague at the manager's discretion. Bereavement leave may be taken non-consecutively within 30 days of the death to accommodate travel, services, or estate obligations. Proof of death, such as an obituary or death certificate, may be requested for absences of more than three days. Additional unpaid leave beyond these limits may be requested through the HR Business Partner and is evaluated case by case with consideration for the employee's circumstances, team capacity, and business needs. Jury Duty and Civic Obligations Apex pays full base salary for employees called to jury duty or subpoenaed as a witness in a legal proceeding unrelated to Apex business. Monitoring, Reporting, and Variance Management Finance business partners publish monthly actual-versus-budget reports for each cost center on the fifth business day after the accounting close. The report shows the current-month and year-to-date actuals versus plan, the full-year outlook based on current run rate, and the ending cash position. Cost center owners are expected to review the report within three business days of publication and submit a variance memo for any line item that is more than 10% or $25,000 off plan, whichever threshold is greater. Apex Systems Financial Reporting Policy Effective January 1, 2026 This policy defines the schedule, content, internal controls, and distribution standards for all financial reporting produced by Apex Systems. It applies to the Corporate Accounting team, the Financial Planning and Analysis team, the Revenue Operations team, and the Tax and Regulatory team. It also applies to any functional leader or department head who reviews, approves, or relies on financial data for decision-making. Accounting Framework and Standards Apex Systems prepares its consolidated financial statements in accordance with United States Generally Accepted Accounting Principles as issued by the Financial Accounting Standards Board. The company is a private entity and is not currently required to file periodic reports with the Securities and Exchange Commission. However, the company operates as if it were subject to SEC reporting standards in preparation for a future initial public offering or significant financing transaction. This includes maintaining internal controls, audit-committee oversight, and disclosure standards consistent with a registered reporting company. The independent external auditor is engaged annually through a competitive RFP process. The audit committee of the board of directors, which consists of at least two independent directors with financial expertise, selects the auditor and approves the annual engagement letter. The external auditor issues an audit opinion on the annual consolidated financial statements; a review opinion is issued for each quarter-end balance sheet and income statement, except the fourth quarter, which is covered by the annual audit. Financial statements include the consolidated balance sheet, the consolidated statement of operations, the consolidated statement of cash flows, the consolidated statement of changes in stockholders' equity, and the notes to the financial statements. The notes include, at minimum: summary of significant accounting policies, revenue disaggregation, stock-based compensation, lease obligations, commitments and contingencies, related-party transactions, and segment information. Monthly Accounting Close The monthly close cycle is designed to produce accurate, reviewed financial statements within five business days of the last calendar day of the month. Each step in the close process is tracked in the close management system with an owner and a due time. Business day 1: The accounting team reconciles all bank accounts, clears outstanding checks and deposits, and verifies that all cash transactions are recorded. The payroll team confirms that the most recent payroll is fully posted and reconciled to the general ledger. Business day 2: Accounts payable is closed for the prior month. Accruals are created for expenses incurred but not yet invoiced, based on contracts, purchase orders, and manager estimates. The most significant accruals — engineering infrastructure, marketing programs, and professional services — are reviewed by the finance business partner before posting. Business day 3: Revenue operations closes the revenue subledger. Deferred revenue schedules are updated for all new bookings, renewals, and cancellations. Usage-based revenue is calculated from metering data and reconciled to the billing system. Any discrepancies between the billing system and the revenue subledger exceeding $10,000 are flagged and investigated before the revenue numbers are finalized. Business day 4: Intercompany eliminations are applied for any transactions between Apex entities. Foreign currency remeasurement is applied to any balance sheet

A PIP specifies measurable goals, a support plan, and a timeline of 60 to 90 days. Completion of a PIP does not guarantee continued employment but does demonstrate good-faith commitment from both parties. Professional Development Every employee receives an annual learning and development budget of $2,000, effective on their hire anniversary date. The budget may be applied to external conferences, online courses, in-person training, books, professional certifications, or executive coaching. Manager approval is required for individual expenses above $500. The budget does not carry over from year to year and cannot be cashed out. Apex sponsors internal knowledge sharing through Friday Tech Talks, a 45-minute session hosted by a volunteer employee or external speaker every Friday at noon Pacific. Recordings are posted to the internal learning library. The company holds an annual Engineering Summit in Q3, which all engineers and product managers are expected to attend regardless of location. Employees pursuing advanced degrees relevant to their role may apply for tuition reimbursement of up to $5,250 per year, the IRS excludable maximum. Applications are reviewed by the HR Business Partner and require evidence of enrollment and a grade of B or better for reimbursement. Code of Conduct Apex is committed to a workplace free from harassment, discrimination, and retaliation. These behaviors are grounds for immediate termination regardless of seniority. Protected characteristics include race, color, religion, sex, national origin, age, disability, genetic information, sexual orientation, gender identity, and veteran status. Apex maintains an anonymous reporting channel, EthicsPoint, operated by a third-party vendor. Reports are reviewed by the Head of HR and the General Counsel. All complaints are investigated within five business days by an HR Business Partner not associated with the reporting chain. The outcome of the investigation is communicated to the reporting party to the extent permitted by law. Employees who witness misconduct are encouraged to report it. Bystander inaction, particularly by people managers, is treated as a contributing factor in misconduct investigations. Conflicts of interest must be disclosed to the HR Business Partner. Common conflicts include ownership stakes in competitor or customer companies, outside board seats, and employment of immediate family members. Disclosed conflicts are reviewed and mitigated, not automatically disqualifying. Equipment and IT Apex provides a company laptop, external monitor, keyboard, and mouse. Employees select their preferred laptop at hire from three options: MacBook Pro 14-inch (Apple silicon), ThinkPad X1 Carbon, or Dell XPS 15. Peripherals are refreshed on a four-year cycle. Additional equipment requests are reviewed by IT with manager approval. Personal use of company equipment is permitted in moderation, provided it does not introduce security risk. Software installed outside of the IT-approved catalog requires a security review, completed by submitting a request through the IT portal. Unapproved software discovered on company devices is removed without notice. Loss or damage must be reported to the IT Help Desk within 24 hours. The OPA instance receives the JWT from the incoming request and evaluates the request against the current policy bundle, which includes three built-in roles (administrator, analyst, viewer) and any custom roles defined by the tenant. Policy bundles are distributed by a bundle server and refreshed every 60 seconds. JWTs issued to customer users are valid for 15 minutes. Refresh tokens are stored as HTTP-only cookies with a 30-day lifetime and are rotated on each use. API keys are long-lived bearer tokens scoped to one or more roles and revocable through the control plane API. Observability Every service emits structured JSON logs to standard output. The Fluent Bit DaemonSet on each node collects logs and ships them to Datadog, where they are indexed and searchable by service, tenant, severity, and correlation_id. Log retention is 90 days in Datadog. All logs containing customer data are tagged with the tenant_id and restricted to Apex staff with the data-access role in Datadog. Application metrics are collected by Prometheus and exposed on a standard /metrics endpoint. A regional Prometheus federation server aggregates metrics across all clusters and makes them available to Grafana dashboards and to the PagerDuty alerting integration. Alerts are tuned to the per-plane SLO targets to minimize noise. Distributed traces are instrumented with OpenTelemetry SDKs across all services. Traces are sampled at 10% for normal traffic and at 100% for requests that result in errors or exceed the 95th-percentile latency threshold. Trace data is shipped to Datadog APM. The trace context propagation header is W3C Trace Context, and all services are required to forward this header in outbound requests. Reliability Targets and Error Budgets The platform publishes formal SLOs in the customer-facing service agreement. The control plane targets 99.9% monthly availability, equivalent to 43.8 minutes of allowed downtime per month. The query plane targets 99.95% monthly availability, equivalent to 21.9 minutes per month. Availability is measured as the percentage of successful synthetic probes issued every 30 seconds from an external monitoring service. Error budgets are calculated monthly and reviewed at the reliability review on the first Wednesday of each month. When a plane's error budget is more than 50% consumed before the end of the month, non-critical deployments for that plane are frozen until the budget is restored or the month resets. When the budget is fully consumed, all deployments to the affected plane require VP of Engineering approval. Data Isolation and Tenant Boundaries Tenant data is strictly isolated at every layer. In the storage plane, each tenant's data resides in a dedicated S3 prefix within a shared bucket, with bucket policy conditions that reject cross-prefix access. In the query plane, Trino session properties are populated from the validated JWT and enforce row-level security policies that restrict each query to the requesting tenant's data. Cross-tenant queries are rejected at the planner level before any execution begins. The control plane performs a double authorization check: the OPA sidecar evaluates the tenant claim in the JWT, and the application layer independently validates that the requested resource belongs to the same tenant. This defense-in-depth approach ensures that a

Apex Systems Expense and Travel Guidelines Effective January 1, 2026 These guidelines govern how Apex Systems employees incur and report business expenses. They apply to all full-time and part-time employees, contractors with an active expense profile, and board members traveling on Apex business. Questions not covered in this document should be directed to the finance business partner for the employee's department before the expense is incurred. Expense Management System All expenses must be submitted through the Navan platform, which is available as a web application and a mobile app. Navan is the single system of record for expense reporting. Expenses submitted through any other channel are not eligible for reimbursement. Expenses must be submitted within 30 calendar days of the transaction date. Expenses submitted between 31 and 60 days after the transaction require a written explanation submitted alongside the report; Finance reviews these on a case-by-case basis. Expenses submitted more than 60 days after the transaction date are not reimbursable under any circumstances without prior written approval from the VP of Finance obtained before the expense was incurred. Receipts are required for any individual transaction of $25 or more. Receipts must be itemized for restaurant and catering expenses — a credit card slip showing only the total is not sufficient. Digital receipts, photos of paper receipts, and PDF attachments are all accepted in Navan. Navan's SmartScan feature can extract the merchant, date, and amount automatically but should be reviewed for accuracy before submission. Each expense report must include a business purpose that identifies the customer, project, or internal meeting that justified the expense. A business purpose of "team lunch" or "travel" is not sufficient. An acceptable business purpose is "lunch with engineering team during Q1 planning offsite, 8 attendees" or "airfare to San Francisco for contract negotiation meeting with Redwood Capital." Reimbursable Expense Categories Travel: airfare, train tickets, rideshare, taxis, airport shuttles, standard rental cars, parking at customer sites, and lodging. Travel costs must be booked through Navan to benefit from pre-negotiated corporate rates. Booking outside Navan is permitted only when the Navan rate is demonstrably higher than a market rate on the same day; the employee must document the comparison in the expense report. Meals: meals consumed while traveling away from the employee's primary work location, meals during customer-facing visits including prospect and customer meetings, meals during interviews with candidates, and working meals during all-day internal planning sessions. Each meal expense must list all attendees and their affiliations. Customer entertainment: client dinners, customer events, sporting events, and other hospitality provided to customers or prospects in the context of a business relationship. Anti-bribery policies of customer organizations must be reviewed before incurring entertainment expenses on their behalf; some government and healthcare customers prohibit receiving gifts or hospitality entirely. A list of all attendees and their affiliations is required. Software and subscriptions: tools directly required for the employee's work that are not already available through the centralized software catalog managed by IT. All software purchases require prior approval from the IT Help Desk before purchase. Submitting an expense for software that was not

The employee must submit a copy of the court summons to the payroll team through the HR portal before the first day of service. Any payment received from the court for jury service may be retained by the employee. Employees are expected to report to work on days when they are released from jury duty before 15:00 local time. On days they are dismissed before noon, they are expected to work for the remainder of the afternoon if feasible. Military leave is provided in accordance with the Uniformed Services Employment and Reemployment Rights Act. Employees returning from qualifying military service are reinstated to the same or an equivalent position with all seniority, pay, and benefits restored as if the employee had not been absent. Sabbatical After five continuous years of full-time service, employees are eligible for a four-week paid sabbatical. The sabbatical is intended for personal renewal, exploration, or rest and is not intended for secondary employment, competitive activities, or consulting. After ten years of continuous service, employees become eligible for an eight-week paid sabbatical. Sabbatical requests must be submitted to the employee's manager and HR Business Partner at least 90 days before the intended start date. The department head and HR Business Partner jointly approve or defer the request. Deferral of more than 180 days requires CFO approval and compensation of one week of additional PTO per 90-day deferral period. Sabbaticals must be taken as a continuous block and may not be split. Sabbatical time does not accrue PTO. Benefits continue during sabbatical at no additional cost to the employee. Unused sabbatical eligibility is forfeited on separation and is not paid out. Company Holidays Apex observes 11 paid holidays each calendar year. Employees working in the United States receive: New Year's Day (January 1), Martin Luther King Jr. Day (third Monday in January), Presidents' Day (third Monday in February), Memorial Day (last Monday in May), Juneteenth (June 19), Independence Day (July 4), Labor Day (first Monday in September), Thanksgiving Day (fourth Thursday in November), the day after Thanksgiving (Friday), Christmas Eve (December 24), and Christmas Day (December 25). When a holiday falls on a Saturday, it is observed on the preceding Friday. When it falls on a Sunday, it is observed on the following Monday. Employees required to work on a company holiday because of customer coverage, on-call rotation, or operational necessity receive a compensatory day off to be taken within the same quarter. Employees in jurisdictions with additional statutory holidays receive those days in addition to the 11 company holidays listed above. A schedule of regional holiday additions is published annually in the HR portal. Leave Request Procedure PTO and sick leave are requested through the HR portal under the Time Off section. Immediate managers are notified automatically upon submission. For planned PTO requests longer than 5 consecutive days, the manager has 5 business days to approve or request a

Applications that meet minimum qualifications are moved to the review stage. The recruiter screen is a thirty-minute video or phone call conducted by the recruiter. The purpose is to confirm the candidate's interest in the role, validate basic qualifications, discuss compensation expectations, and assess communication and professionalism. Hiring Manager Screen The hiring manager screen is a forty-five-minute video call between the candidate and the hiring manager. The purpose is to explore the candidate's background and experience in more depth, assess alignment with team needs, and give the candidate a clear picture of the role and team. The hiring manager should review the candidate's resume and the recruiter's notes before the call. Hiring managers are expected to ask consistent, competency-based questions for all candidates interviewing for the same role. People Operations maintains a question bank organized by competency and level that hiring managers must draw from. Follow-up or clarifying questions are permitted. Hypothetical or brain-teaser questions that are not grounded in real work scenarios are discouraged. Questions about protected characteristics including age, marital status, family plans, religion, national origin, and disability are prohibited. After the hiring manager screen, the hiring manager provides a written recommendation within twenty-four hours through the applicant tracking system. Candidates who advance proceed to the formal interview loop. Technical Interview For engineering, data, and product roles, a technical interview is required before the full panel interview loop. The technical interview is designed and administered by the relevant technical team. Technical interviewers must submit written feedback in the applicant tracking system within twenty-four hours of the interview. Apex Systems Deployment Guide Revision 2.1 — January 2026 This guide is the authoritative reference for shipping code to any environment operated by Apex Systems. It applies to every engineer who merges code to the monorepo, regardless of seniority or team. Deviations from this process require written approval from the VP of Engineering and are documented in the exceptions log. Environments Apex operates four environments with distinct purposes, access controls, and deployment rules. Local is the development environment that every engineer runs on their workstation. It is defined as a docker-compose profile in the monorepo root. Local spins up all services, an in-process Kafka broker, a PostgreSQL instance, a MinIO S3-compatible object store, and a lightweight Trino container. Engineers are expected to keep local fully functional; a broken local environment that is not fixed within 24 hours should be escalated to the DevEx team. Seed data scripts are included for each service so that engineers do not interact with staging data. Staging is a full Kubernetes environment hosted in us-east-1 that mirrors the production topology, including all three worker node groups, the full observability stack, and real SSL certificates. Staging serves synthetic data generated by a daily data generator job. Engineers use staging for integration testing, external demos, and validating infrastructure changes. Staging is shared across the team; destructive tests that affect shared state must be coordinated in the #staging-coordination Slack channel. Canary is a 5% traffic slice of the production cluster. A separate Kubernetes service revision is deployed, and traffic is split using Argo Rollouts with a weighted VirtualService in Istio. Canary exists to validate behavioral changes under real customer traffic before full promotion. Engineers may observe canary metrics in the Grafana canary dashboard, which is automatically pinned to the revision deployed. Production serves 100% of customer traffic across three regions. Deployment to production is fully automated after a successful canary period. Engineers do not deploy to production manually; the delivery pipeline handles promotion. Source Control and Code Review All services, libraries, infrastructure definitions, Helm charts, and CI configuration live in a single monorepo hosted on GitHub. The monorepo uses Bazel for builds with a shared build cache hosted on a dedicated S3 bucket. Bazel ensures that only affected targets are rebuilt and retested, which keeps CI times bounded even as the repository grows. Developers branch from main using the naming convention owner-username/APEX-ticket-number-short-description. Branches older than 30 days without an open pull request are automatically deleted by a nightly GitHub Actions workflow. Direct pushes to main are disallowed by branch protection rules. Every change requires a pull request. Pull requests require: at least one approval from a person listed in the CODEOWNERS file for the changed path, passing required status checks, no unresolved review comments marked as blocking, and no merge conflicts. Force-pushing to open PRs is disallowed. Approved PRs are merged using the squash-and-merge strategy to keep the main branch history linear. For changes that affect the API contract, the Helm values schema, the Kafka message schema, or the database migration files, a second approval from the platform-owners team is

a prorated bonus based on months worked. Sales Compensation Sales roles — Account Executive, Sales Development Representative, Sales Engineer, and Customer Success Manager — operate on individual sales compensation plans issued at the start of each fiscal year or at hire. Plans specify the base salary, on-target variable, quota, and accelerator and decelerator structure. Standard structures are either 50/50 base-to-variable for pure quota-carrying roles or 60/40 for roles with a mix of quota and relationship responsibilities. Commissions are calculated monthly against booked, signed, and invoiced revenue that meets qualification criteria defined in the plan. Accelerators apply at 100%, 120%, and 150% of quota attainment. Revenue booked above 150% of quarterly quota earns at 175% of the standard commission rate. Decelerators apply below 50% attainment in a given quarter, resulting in a 50% commission rate. The Finance and Revenue Operations teams administer commission calculations and publish results in Spiff by the fifth business day after month end. Plan disputes must be raised with the Revenue Operations team within 15 days of statement publication. Disputes filed after this window are reviewed at the company's discretion. Equity All full-time employees receive a stock option grant at hire. Options are priced at the fair market value established by the most recent 409A valuation conducted by a third-party qualified appraiser. Grants vest over four years: 25% vest after the first year of service (the one-year cliff), and the remaining 75% vest monthly over the following 36 months at a rate of 1/48th of the total grant per month. New-hire grant sizes by level for Tier 1 locations, as a percentage of options representing the fully diluted equity: L1 and L2: 0.01% to 0.04% L3 Senior: 0.05% to 0.10% L4 Staff: 0.10% to 0.20% L5 Principal: 0.20% to 0.40% L6 Director: 0.30% to 0.60% L7 Vice President: individually negotiated, typically 0.5% to 1.5% Refresh grants are awarded each September to employees in good standing, based on performance and tenure. Target refresh amounts equal approximately 25% of the annualized new-hire grant for the employee's current level. Refresh grants are subject to the same four-year vesting schedule with a one-year cliff. There is no limit on the number of refresh grants an employee may receive. Employees have 90 days following their termination date to exercise vested options. Unvested options are forfeited upon separation. Employees who resign with more than four years of service and at least 12 months remaining on their final refresh grant may request an extension of the exercise window to 12 months; this requires CEO approval. Merit Increases and Promotions Base salary increases are processed in the September compensation cycle for all eligible employees. Increases are effective October 1. To be eligible for a merit increase, an employee must have been in their current role for at least six months and must have received a performance rating of developing or better. Employees rated developing typically receive no merit increase. Typical merit increase ranges by rating: exceptional — 8% to 12%; exceeds expectations — 5% to 8%; meets expectations — 3% to 5%; developing — 0%. Promotions carry

(ARR), defined as the annualized value of all active subscription contracts as of the measurement date, excluding one-time fees and services. Net new ARR, the change in ARR from one period to the next. Gross dollar retention (GDR), the percentage of beginning ARR retained from existing customers excluding expansions, measured annually. Net dollar retention (NDR), the percentage of beginning ARR retained including expansions and contractions from existing customers, measured annually. Customer acquisition cost (CAC), total sales and marketing expense divided by the number of new customers acquired. Magic number, the net new ARR generated per dollar of sales and marketing spend in the prior period. Rule of 40, the sum of the trailing-12-month revenue growth rate and the EBITDA margin, indicating efficiency. Free cash flow is defined as cash from operations minus capital expenditures, including capitalized internal-use software development costs. Burn multiple is the net cash burned per dollar of net new ARR added, a measure of capital efficiency. Board Reporting The monthly board flash report is distributed on the eighth business day after month end. The audit committee reviews the control framework annually and receives a control effectiveness report from the internal audit function. Segregation of duties: No single employee may initiate, approve, and record a financial transaction. Approval authority in the accounting system is enforced at the role level; engineers and support staff do not have access to financial systems. Journal entries require a preparer and a separate approver. Manual journal entries above $25,000 require the Controller's approval. Journal entry controls: all journal entries are logged with a preparer ID, a timestamp, and a supporting document reference. A management report of all manual journal entries is produced monthly and reviewed by the Controller. Recurring journal entries are documented in a recurring entry schedule and approved quarterly. Reconciliation controls: every balance sheet account is reconciled monthly to a subledger or external source. Reconciliations are signed off by the preparer and a second reviewer. Unreconciled items over $5,000 that

PATCH /users/{user_id} updates a user's role or active status. DELETE /users/{user_id} deactivates the user and revokes all active sessions; the user record is retained for audit purposes. Role management: GET /roles returns the built-in roles (administrator, analyst, viewer) and all customer-defined roles. POST /roles creates a custom role with a name, a description, and an array of permission strings. PATCH /roles/{role_id} updates the permission set of a custom role; the change propagates to all active sessions within 60 seconds via the OPA policy bundle refresh cycle. API key management: POST /api-keys issues a new API key for a service account. The request body includes a name, a role, and an optional expiry date. The response includes the key_id and the plaintext secret in a single_use_secret field. The plaintext secret is shown exactly once and is not stored by Apex. GET /api-keys lists all active keys with their names, roles, creation dates, and last-used timestamps. DELETE /api-keys/{key_id} immediately revokes the key. Tenant configuration: GET /tenant returns the current tenant configuration, including the identity provider settings, rate limit tiers, data retention policy, and feature flags enabled for the tenant. PATCH /tenant updates mutable configuration fields. Multi-region replication settings are not mutable through the API; contact support for changes. Webhooks Apex delivers event notifications to customer-configured HTTP endpoints. Supported event types are: ingestion.completed, ingestion.failed, schema.changed, query.completed (for async jobs), audit.event, and billing.threshold_reached. Webhook subscriptions are created with POST /webhooks. The request body specifies the endpoint URL (HTTPS only), an array of event types to subscribe to, and a human-readable description. The response includes the webhook_id and a webhook_secret used to verify payloads. Every webhook request includes the headers X-Apex-Webhook-Id, X-Apex-Event-Type, X-Apex-Delivery-Id, and X-Apex-Timestamp (Unix epoch seconds, UTC). Payloads are signed with HMAC-SHA256 using the webhook secret. The signature is in the X-Apex-Signature-256 header and is computed over the raw request body. Receiver implementations must verify the signature before processing the payload and must reject requests where the X-Apex-Timestamp is more than 5 minutes in the past to prevent replay attacks. Apex delivers webhook events with at-least-once semantics. Receivers must be idempotent; the X-Apex-Delivery-Id header provides a stable identifier per event occurrence that can be used for deduplication. Apex retries delivery up to 5 times with exponential backoff (1, 5, 25, 125, 625 seconds) if the receiver returns any non-2xx response. After 5 failures, the event is written to the webhook dead-letter log accessible through GET /webhooks/{webhook_id}/failures. Pagination All list endpoints use cursor-based pagination. When a response contains more results than the requested page_size, the response body includes a next_cursor field containing an opaque string. Pass this value as the cursor query parameter in the next request. The default page size is 25; the maximum is 200. Cursor values are valid for 24 hours; requests with an expired cursor receive HTTP 400 with a cursor_expired type. Offset-based pagination is not supported, as it produces inconsistent results on

Apex Systems Leave Policy Effective January 1, 2026 This document defines all categories of paid and unpaid leave available to Apex Systems employees. It applies to all regular full-time employees based in the United States. Employees working outside the United States should consult the local addendum maintained by the HR Business Partner for their region, which may provide different or additional entitlements required by local law. Paid Time Off Full-time employees accrue PTO at the rate of 1.67 days per calendar month, for a total of 20 days per year. Accrual begins on the first day of employment and is credited at the start of each calendar month. Part-time employees working at least 20 hours per week accrue PTO on a prorated basis. PTO encompasses vacation, personal days, and any other discretionary time away from work that does not fall into one of the specific categories below. Employees are encouraged to use their PTO throughout the year to maintain work-life balance. Managers are responsible for proactively planning coverage to ensure employees can take leave without jeopardizing customer commitments. PTO may be taken in minimum increments of half a day. Requests of less than three consecutive days require at least three business days of advance notice. Requests of three or more consecutive days require manager approval submitted through the HR portal at least two weeks before the intended start date. Up to five unused PTO days may be carried over into the following calendar year. Any accrual above the five-day carryover cap is forfeited on December 31. Employees who were unable to use PTO due to business-critical needs may apply to their HR Business Partner for an exception carryover of up to five additional days, subject to VP approval. Employees leaving the company are paid out for all accrued but unused PTO in their final paycheck, subject to the laws of the employee's home state. California employees are paid out at 100% regardless of any forfeiture provisions above; other states vary. Sick Leave Sick leave is provided separately from PTO at 10 days per year, front-loaded on January 1. New hires receive a prorated sick leave balance on their start date based on the number of months remaining in the calendar year. Sick leave may be used for: personal illness or injury, medical or dental appointments, mental health days, preventive care, and care for an immediate family member who is ill, injured, or attending a medical appointment. Absences of three consecutive days or more require a note from a licensed healthcare provider confirming the employee's inability to work and the anticipated return date. The note does not need to include a diagnosis. Apex does not require medical notes for absences of one or two days. Unused sick leave does not carry over from year to year and is not paid out on separation. In states that mandate sick leave accrual rather than front-loading, the policy is administered in compliance with the applicable state law. pricing tier — Standard, Professional, and Enterprise — runs a dedicated Trino cluster to provide performance isolation. Enterprise cluster nodes are provisioned at r6i.xlarge, allowing larger working sets for complex joins and window functions. Query planning is enhanced by a cost-based optimizer that is calibrated nightly. The calibration job samples recent query history, measures actual execution statistics, and updates table statistics stored in the Glue catalog. This keeps the optimizer's cardinality estimates accurate as data volumes grow. The query proxy service sits in front of Trino and enforces per-tenant query concurrency limits, maximum query execution time (10 minutes), and circuit breakers that reject queries when the cluster utilization exceeds 90%. Long-running queries that exceed the time limit are terminated with an informative error; customers are directed to the asynchronous jobs endpoint for analytics that require more than 10 minutes. A Redis-backed result cache stores the outputs of deterministic queries — those with no volatile functions and no row-level security filters that vary by user — for a configurable TTL defined per tenant. Cache keys incorporate the query text, the table version identifier, and the tenant_id. A cache hit for a dashboard tile delivers the result in under 50 milliseconds. Control Plane The control plane is a Go-based service that manages the cross-cutting concerns of the entire platform: tenant provisioning, user management, role definitions, API key issuance, billing metering, audit log aggregation, and system health dashboards. It is the system of record for all configuration state. The control plane is backed by Aurora PostgreSQL with multi-AZ failover. The primary instance handles all writes; read replicas in the same region serve read-heavy operations such as user lookup and API key validation. The Aurora cluster is sized at db.r6g.xlarge with storage autoscaling enabled. A connection pool managed by PgBouncer keeps the number of database connections bounded regardless of the number of application pods. All write operations to the control plane produce an audit event containing the actor, the resource type and ID, the action, the before and after state, a timestamp, and a cryptographic hash of the event chain. Events are signed and appended to an immutable CloudWatch Logs stream. Customers with Enterprise contracts have read access to their own audit log stream and may export it to their SIEM. Authentication and Authorization Authentication uses OpenID Connect. Supported identity providers are Okta, Microsoft Azure Active Directory, and Google Workspace. Customers configure their identity provider through the control plane UI; the Apex service registers as an OIDC relying party and validates tokens against the provider's discovery endpoint. Employees and internal service accounts authenticate through Okta. Authorization is enforced by an Open Policy Agent sidecar co-deployed with every service that handles customer data. The OPA instance receives the JWT from the incoming request and evaluates the request against the current policy bundle, which includes three built-in roles (administrator, analyst, viewer) and any custom roles defined by the tenant. Policy bundles are distributed by a bundle server and refreshed every 60 seconds. JWTs issued to customer users are valid for 15 minutes. Refresh tokens are stored as HTTP-only

Apex Systems Compensation Guide Effective January 1, 2026 This guide explains how Apex Systems structures base pay, variable compensation, equity, and benefits for all full-time employees. It is distributed at hire, republished annually after the September compensation review, and updated mid-year if material changes are made. Managers are encouraged to walk through this guide with new team members during the first month of employment. Compensation Philosophy Apex targets the 65th percentile of base salary for our peer group, defined as venture-backed enterprise software companies with between 200 and 2,000 employees and annual recurring revenue between $50 million and $500 million, primarily based in the United States. Total target compensation — including base salary, target annual bonus, and the annualized Black-Scholes value of equity grants — is positioned at the 75th percentile of the same peer group. Pay is benchmarked twice per year using Radford Global Technology Survey data, with a supplemental reference to Levels.fyi for engineering roles to capture real-world market movement in technical talent. When survey data and market anecdotes diverge by more than 10% at a given level, HR initiates an ad hoc market review before the next scheduled calibration. The philosophy is grounded in the belief that compensating people well relative to the market reduces voluntary attrition, eliminates a significant source of distraction, and allows the company to attract and retain people who would otherwise join larger competitors. Career Levels and Pay Bands Apex operates a single unified level framework that applies across all functions — engineering, product, design, sales, finance, HR, legal, and operations. The seven levels and their approximate base salary ranges for Tier 1 locations are: L1 Associate: $70,000 to $90,000. Entry-level roles. Typically 0 to 2 years of professional experience. Supervised closely with structured deliverables. L2 Specialist: $90,000 to $120,000. Independently executes well-defined tasks. Typically 2 to 4 years of experience. Contributes to team planning and improves their own work quality without being asked. L3 Senior: $120,000 to $160,000. Drives outcomes for a defined scope of work with limited oversight. Mentors L1 and L2 colleagues informally. Typically 4 to 8 years of experience. This is the career level where most employees spend the majority of their tenure. L4 Staff: $160,000 to $210,000. Owns complex cross-team problems and drives alignment across peers. Influences roadmap and architecture. Typically requires significant domain expertise and strong cross-functional communication. L5 Principal: $210,000 to $280,000. Sets technical or functional direction for a broad domain. Operates with VP-level influence and visibility. Expected to develop L3 and L4 talent. Fewer than 5% of employees reach this level. L6 Director: $250,000 to $350,000. Manages teams or functions, sets strategy, represents the domain to senior leadership. Accountable for team performance, hiring, and culture. L7 Vice President: $350,000 and above. Leads a major function or business unit. Member of the senior leadership team. Compensation for L7 is individually negotiated with CEO approval and CFO review. Each band has a published minimum, midpoint, and maximum. The midpoint represents the market-competitive rate for a fully proficient, high-performing employee at that level. Employees newly promoted typically enter between the
