Tier 1 locations, set at 100% of the published band, include: San Francisco Bay Area, New York City metropolitan area, and Seattle metropolitan area. Tier 2 locations, set at 95% of the published band, include: Boston, Washington DC metropolitan area, Los Angeles, San Diego, Chicago, Austin, Denver, and Miami. Tier 3 locations, set at 90% of the published band, cover all other U.S. locations including fully remote employees outside Tier 1 and Tier 2 cities. Employees in Tier 3 cities still participate in the same level framework and bonus and equity programs. International employees are compensated using local market benchmarks and are not subject to the U.S. geographic differential table. Variable Pay — Performance Bonus Non-sales, non-executive employees at L1 through L5 are eligible for an annual performance bonus with an on-target value of 10% of base salary. L6 Directors have a target bonus of 20%. L7 Vice Presidents have a target of 30%, as individually negotiated. Bonus payouts are calculated as the product of a company performance multiplier and an individual performance multiplier. The company multiplier ranges from 0 to 1.5 based on achievement of the annual plan; achievement below 70% of plan results in a 0 multiplier. The individual multiplier ranges from 0 to 1.5 based on the employee's September performance rating: exceptional earns 1.5, exceeds expectations earns 1.2, meets expectations earns 1.0, developing earns 0.5, and unsatisfactory earns 0. The two multipliers are applied to the target bonus amount, capping total bonus at 200% of target. Bonuses are paid in March of the following year. To be eligible, an employee must be active on February 28 of the payment year and must have been employed for at least six months of the preceding performance year. Employees who were on approved leave for part of the year receive a prorated bonus based on months worked. Sales Compensation Sales roles — Account Executive, Sales Development Representative, Sales Engineer, and Customer Success Manager — operate on individual sales compensation plans issued at the start of each fiscal year or at hire. Plans specify the base salary, on-target variable, quota, and accelerator and decelerator structure. Standard structures are either 50/50 base-to-variable for pure quota-carrying roles

The Helm chart for the changed service is updated with the new image tag, committed to the gitops repository, and synced by Argo CD within two minutes. Deployment to canary is gated by a human approval step in the Argo Workflow. The engineer who merged the change, or the on-call engineer, clicks Approve in the Argo UI after verifying that staging tests have passed. Canary deployments are permitted only during business hours (09:00 to 17:00 Pacific Monday through Friday) unless the change is a rollback of an active incident. The canary revision receives 5% of production traffic, held for 30 minutes. Promotion from canary to production is automatic if no SLO-based alerts have fired during the canary window. Promotion triggers a blue-green deployment in each of the three production regions, sequenced east-to-west with a 5-minute wait between regions. If an SLO alert fires at any point during the canary window or during production rollout, the delivery pipeline halts and pages the on-call engineer. Feature Flags All changes that modify user-visible behavior, change a data schema, or affect customer-facing performance characteristics must be gated behind a LaunchDarkly feature flag before merging. Flags default to off in all environments. The flag name must match the Jira ticket number using the format APEX-{number}-{short-description}. Flags are enabled progressively: first for internal Apex accounts, then for a 1% cohort of tenants, then 10%, then 50%, then 100%. Each cohort expansion is a manual step in LaunchDarkly performed by the feature owner after verifying that error rates, latency, and business metrics are nominal at the current cohort size. A flag that reaches 100% rollout and has been stable for 30 days must be removed from the codebase. The DevEx team publishes a weekly report of flags eligible for removal. Flags that are not removed within 60 days of eligibility are tracked as technical debt in the reliability review. Secrets and Configuration Management Secrets — API keys, database passwords, signing keys, OAuth credentials — are stored in AWS Secrets Manager. The External Secrets Operator watches Kubernetes CRDs that reference Secrets Manager paths and materializes the secret values as Kubernetes Secret objects at pod startup. Secrets are never printed in logs; log sanitization middleware strips values matching known patterns. Application configuration — feature defaults, resource limits, log levels, timeouts — uses Helm values organized in a layered structure: base values defined in the chart, environment-specific overlays committed to the gitops repository, and per-tenant dynamic configuration served by the control plane configuration API at runtime. Engineers should prefer the control plane API for any configuration that must change frequently or differ by tenant. Secrets and configuration are never baked into container images. Any image layer that contains a secret is considered compromised and triggers a rotation incident. An accidental commit of a plaintext secret to the repository triggers an automated response: the secret is flagged by the GitHub secret scanning integration, the rotation

Software installed outside of the IT-approved catalog requires a security review, completed by submitting a request through the IT portal. Unapproved software discovered on company devices is removed without notice. Loss or damage must be reported to the IT Help Desk within 24 hours. Damage attributable to negligence may be charged to the employee at the depreciated value of the equipment. Theft requires a police report filed within 24 hours. Employees accessing Apex systems from personal devices must enroll the device in mobile device management and use only approved applications to access company data. Unenrolled personal devices are blocked from accessing internal systems. Confidentiality and Data Handling All employees sign a confidentiality and invention assignment agreement at hire. The agreement covers customer data, technical designs, business plans, financial information, and any information designated as confidential by a manager or the legal team. Customer data may only be accessed from company-managed devices and through approved channels. Screenshots, exports, or copies of customer data to personal devices or unauthorized cloud storage are strictly prohibited. Production database access requires a separate access request and is logged and audited. Queries to production data must be reviewed by a peer and documented with a business justification. Violations of data handling policy are reported to the Chief Information Security Officer and are subject to disciplinary action up to and including termination. Violations that may constitute a breach of customer data trigger the incident response plan and may require regulatory notification. Attendance and Absence Unplanned absences must be reported to the direct manager before the start of the workday via Slack or email. If the manager is unreachable, the team channel should be notified. Three consecutive unexplained absences are treated as voluntary job abandonment, and the employment relationship is considered terminated. Employees who anticipate a recurring pattern of lateness or absence due to a medical condition, disability, or family care obligation should contact the HR Business Partner to discuss an accommodation or an adjusted schedule before the pattern becomes a performance issue. Termination and Resignation Employees are asked to provide two weeks of written notice delivered to their manager and HR. Apex may accept notice and elect to end employment sooner with compensation through the notice period, or Apex may ask the employee to remain active through the full notice period depending on business need. Senior and technical roles may be asked to provide additional transition documentation. HR schedules an exit interview with the HR Business Partner on the final day of employment or via video call for remote employees. The exit interview covers departure reasons, feedback about the team and management experience, and transition status. Company equipment, physical badges, and credentials are collected or remotely wiped during the offboarding session. Final pay, including any accrued and unused PTO, is disbursed in accordance with applicable state law, typically on the next standard pay date or sooner if required. Employees with outstanding expense reports should submit them before their final day to ensure reimbursement. Policy questions should be directed

a customer-managed KMS key, and a processing job is enqueued. Streaming events are delivered through a webhook endpoint that writes messages to a per-tenant Kafka partition in near real time. The ingestion workers, written in Rust, consume from Kafka topics and process records in parallel. Each worker validates records against the tenant-defined schema stored in the schema registry, applies PII detection rules configured per tenant, strips or masks sensitive fields, and serializes clean records as Parquet files. Validated records are published to the normalized Kafka topic. Records that fail schema validation or exceed the size limit are routed to a tenant-specific dead-letter queue with a 14-day retention, after which they are deleted. DLQ events are surfaced in the customer console with a summary of the failure reason. Schema changes are applied through a versioned migration API rather than in-place modification. Each schema version is immutable once published. The ingestion worker uses the schema version embedded in each incoming message to route to the correct validation and serialization logic, allowing old and new versions to coexist during migration windows. Storage Plane Normalized records from the ingestion plane land in an Apache Iceberg data lake stored on S3. Iceberg provides ACID transaction guarantees, partition evolution, and time-travel queries. The Iceberg catalog is managed by a Go service backed by AWS Glue, which tracks table definitions, partition metadata, schema history, and compaction state. The catalog service exposes a gRPC API consumed by both the ingestion workers (for writing) and the Trino query engine (for reading). Hot partitions — those representing the most recent 48 hours of data for any tenant — are cached in Redis using a time-to-live keyed to the partition date. Dashboard tile queries that aggregate over recent partitions are satisfied from cache without hitting S3. The cache invalidation strategy is event-driven: the ingestion worker publishes a cache invalidation message to Kafka after each partition write, and the cache service consumes this to evict or refresh the affected keys. Summary aggregates for common dashboard metrics are precomputed by a scheduled Spark job running every 15 minutes and written to TimescaleDB. TimescaleDB is a PostgreSQL extension optimized for time-series data, and it serves the dashboard backend directly with sub-100-millisecond responses for tenant-scoped aggregate queries. Backups of the Iceberg catalog metadata and the TimescaleDB summaries run hourly using AWS Backup. Catalog metadata is replicated cross-region with a recovery point objective of 15 minutes. Full S3 object-level replication provides an additional layer of data durability. The recovery time objective for a full storage plane failure is 30 minutes for metadata and 4 hours for full data availability. Query Plane Customer analytical queries are executed by Trino, an open-source distributed SQL engine. Each pricing tier — Standard, Professional, and Enterprise — runs a dedicated Trino cluster to provide performance isolation. Enterprise cluster nodes are provisioned at r6i.xlarge, allowing larger working sets for complex joins and window functions. Query planning is enhanced by a cost-based optimizer that is calibrated nightly. Apex Systems Public API — Developer Documentation API version v2 — Revision January 2026 The Apex Systems Public API enables customers to programmatically ingest data, execute analytical queries, manage users and roles, configure webhooks, and monitor audit events. This document is the primary reference for developers integrating with the v2 API. The full machine-readable specification is published as an OpenAPI 3.1 document at https://api.apex-systems.example/openapi.json and is kept in sync with each release. Base URL and Versioning The v2 API is served at https://api.apex-systems.example/v2. All HTTPS requests must use TLS 1.2 or higher. TLS 1.0 and 1.1 connections are rejected at the load balancer. Apex uses semantic versioning for the API. Breaking changes — removal of a field, change to an existing field's type or constraints, change in HTTP status code semantics, removal of an endpoint — always increment the major version. A deprecated major version is maintained for a minimum of 12 months after the successor version reaches general availability, after which it is sunset with 90 days of advance notice via the developer changelog and a deprecation header added to responses. Non-breaking additions — new optional request fields, new response fields, new optional query parameters, new endpoints — may appear in the current version at any time. Clients must be written to tolerate unknown fields in responses. The current API version is included in every response in the X-Apex-Api-Version header. If a client detects that the version in the response differs from the expected version, it should log a warning and test for forward compatibility. Authentication Every API request must include a bearer token in the Authorization HTTP header: Authorization: Bearer {token}. Two token types are supported. API keys are long-lived credentials associated with a service account. They are issued from the Apex administrator console, scoped to one or more roles at issuance time, and remain valid until explicitly revoked. API keys are the recommended authentication method for server-to-server integrations. The key secret is shown exactly once at creation and is not recoverable; if lost, a replacement must be issued and the old key revoked. API keys do not expire automatically but should be rotated on a 90-day schedule as a security best practice. OAuth 2.0 access tokens are obtained through the authorization code flow with PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange), the recommended flow for interactive applications where the user authenticates with their identity provider. The Apex OIDC discovery document is published at https://auth.apex-systems.example/.well-known/openid-configuration. Access tokens are short-lived with a 15-minute expiry. Refresh tokens are issued alongside access tokens and are valid for 30 days, rotated on each use. Unauthenticated requests receive HTTP 401 Unauthorized with a WWW-Authenticate header. Authenticated requests that lack the required role for the requested operation receive HTTP 403 Forbidden. The error response body follows the standard error model described below. Rate Limits The API enforces rate limits at the tenant level. Default limits apply to all tenants unless overridden by a plan-level or contract-level configuration. Default limits: 600 requests per minute across all API keys and access tokens combined, with a burst allowance of 60

passing required status checks, no unresolved review comments marked as blocking, and no merge conflicts. Force-pushing to open PRs is disallowed. Approved PRs are merged using the squash-and-merge strategy to keep the main branch history linear. For changes that affect the API contract, the Helm values schema, the Kafka message schema, or the database migration files, a second approval from the platform-owners team is required in addition to the CODEOWNERS approval. This double-approval gate catches backward-incompatibility issues before they reach staging. Continuous Integration Pipeline CI runs on GitHub Actions using self-hosted runners on c6i.4xlarge EC2 instances provisioned by the runner manager. Self-hosted runners avoid the memory and disk limits of GitHub-hosted runners and allow Bazel's remote cache to be used without network latency. The standard pipeline is triggered on every push to a pull request branch and on every merge to main. The pipeline stages run in order: Stage 1 — Build: Bazel builds all affected targets. The build cache hit rate is monitored; a cache miss rate above 15% triggers a review of the cache key configuration. Stage 2 — Unit tests: Bazel runs all unit tests affected by the changed targets. Tests must complete in under 5 minutes per service; tests that exceed this threshold are flagged for splitting or optimization. Stage 3 — Integration tests: Integration tests run against ephemeral Kind clusters provisioned by the test framework. Each Kind cluster is bootstrapped with the current chart versions and the changed service revision. Integration tests cover the happy path and documented error paths for each service API. Stage 4 — Static analysis: golangci-lint for Go services, ruff for Python, clippy for Rust, eslint for TypeScript. Analysis failures are treated as build failures. Stage 5 — Security scans: Semgrep runs a ruleset covering OWASP Top 10 and Apex custom rules for common misconfigurations. Trivy scans every container image for known CVEs with critical or high severity; images with unfixed critical CVEs are blocked from promotion. The SBOM is generated for every image and stored in the internal artifact registry. Total pipeline duration must remain under 25 minutes. The DevEx team monitors pipeline duration weekly and works with teams to optimize slow stages. Continuous Delivery and Progressive Delivery Merges to main trigger the delivery pipeline via Argo CD. The pipeline is defined as an Argo Workflow that orchestrates build, sign, publish, and deploy stages. Builds are triggered by a push event from GitHub. The container image is built by Kaniko running inside the cluster, avoiding the need for Docker daemon access. The image is signed with cosign using a key stored in AWS KMS. The signed image is pushed to the internal ECR registry. Image signing is verified at deployment time by Kyverno policies; images without a valid signature are rejected by the admission controller. Deployment to staging is fully automatic on every merge to main. The Helm chart for the changed service is updated with the new image tag, committed to the gitops repository, and synced by Argo CD within two minutes. Deployment to canary is gated by a human approval

The submission is processed asynchronously; the submission status can be polled using GET /submissions/{submission_id}. POST /datasets/{dataset_id}/files Creates a pre-signed S3 PUT URL for uploading a file directly to Apex storage without passing through the API servers. Accepted file formats are Parquet, CSV (UTF-8 with configurable delimiter), and newline-delimited JSON. Maximum file size is 5 GB. The request body specifies the filename, format, and expected row count (optional, used for progress reporting). The response contains the pre-signed URL (valid for 2 hours), an upload_id, and the expected Content-Type header to use in the PUT request. After the file is uploaded to the pre-signed URL, the file must be registered by calling POST /datasets/{dataset_id}/files/{upload_id}/complete. This triggers ingestion processing. Progress can be monitored through GET /datasets/{dataset_id}/files/{upload_id}/status. GET /submissions/{submission_id} Returns the current status of a batch ingestion submission. Possible statuses are pending, processing, complete, and failed. A complete response includes the total record count, the accepted count, and the rejected count. A failed response includes a top-level error describing the systemic failure. Schema management: GET /datasets/{dataset_id}/schema returns the current schema version, column definitions, and validation rules. POST /datasets/{dataset_id}/schema creates a new schema version. Schema versions are immutable once a dataset has data written under them; older versions continue to support ingestion until explicitly deprecated by the dataset owner. Query Endpoints POST /datasets/{dataset_id}/query Executes a SQL query against the dataset and streams results using server-sent events (SSE). The request body contains a sql string (maximum 64 KB) and an optional params object for parameterized values. Parameterization is strongly recommended to prevent SQL injection and improve query plan caching. The SSE stream emits one JSON event per row produced by the query, a progress event every 5 seconds for long-running queries, and a final statistics event containing the row count, bytes scanned, execution duration, and cache hit indicator. The stream closes after the statistics event or on error. Queries are subject to a maximum execution time of 10 minutes; queries that exceed this threshold are terminated and the client receives an error event with the reason execution_timeout. GET /jobs For queries expected to exceed 10 minutes, use the asynchronous jobs endpoint. POST /jobs accepts the same request body as POST /query. The response returns a job_id and an estimated duration. The job runs in the background; results are written to a temporary S3 location accessible via GET /jobs/{job_id}/results. Jobs expire 24 hours after completion. Filtering and projection: To avoid transferring unnecessary columns, include a select array of column names in the query request. Server-side projection reduces response size and latency significantly for wide tables. Administration Endpoints User management: POST /users creates a user in the tenant with a specified role. The request body includes email, role, and an optional display_name. A welcome email with a credential setup link is sent automatically. PATCH /users/{user_id} updates a user's role or active status. DELETE /users/{user_id} deactivates the user and revokes all active sessions; the user record is retained for audit purposes. Role management: GET /roles returns the built-in roles

Default limits apply to all tenants unless overridden by a plan-level or contract-level configuration. Default limits: 600 requests per minute across all API keys and access tokens combined, with a burst allowance of 60 requests in any single second. Ingestion endpoints have a separate limit of 100 requests per minute to protect downstream Kafka throughput. Query endpoints have a concurrency limit of 10 simultaneous in-flight queries per tenant. Enterprise plan limits: 3,000 requests per minute and 200 simultaneous queries. Higher limits are available with advance notice; contact your account manager. When the rate limit is reached, the API returns HTTP 429 Too Many Requests. The response includes three headers: X-RateLimit-Limit (the configured limit), X-RateLimit-Remaining (the number of requests remaining in the current window), and Retry-After (the number of seconds until the limit resets). Clients must respect the Retry-After value. Ignoring the retry guidance and polling at high frequency during a rate-limit period may result in temporary IP-level blocking. All SDKs maintained by Apex implement exponential backoff with full jitter starting from the Retry-After value. Third-party integrations should implement the same. Error Model All error responses conform to RFC 7807 Problem Details. The response body is a JSON object with the following fields: type: a URI that identifies the error class, such as https://api.apex-systems.example/errors/schema-validation-failed. These URIs are stable and may be used in client code for programmatic error handling. title: a human-readable summary of the problem type, consistent for all occurrences of the same type. status: the HTTP status code as an integer, included for convenience. detail: a human-readable description of the specific occurrence, including actionable guidance where applicable. instance: a URI that identifies the specific request, useful for tracing in logs. correlation_id: a UUID generated per request. Customers should include this in support tickets. Apex support uses it to locate the request trace in Datadog. For validation errors, an additional errors array is included, where each element identifies the field path that failed validation and the rule that was violated. Ingestion Endpoints POST /datasets/{dataset_id}/records Synchronously validates and asynchronously ingests a batch of JSON records. The request body is a JSON object with a records array of up to 1,000 individual records. Each record is validated against the dataset schema; records that fail validation are rejected and included in the error response without affecting the valid records in the same batch. The maximum size per record is 1 MB; the maximum total request body size is 50 MB. The response returns HTTP 202 Accepted with a body containing a submission_id (UUID), a submitted_count, a rejected_count, and an array of rejection details. The submission is processed asynchronously; the submission status can be polled using GET /submissions/{submission_id}. Any image layer that contains a secret is considered compromised and triggers a rotation incident. An accidental commit of a plaintext secret to the repository triggers an automated response: the secret is flagged by the GitHub secret scanning integration, the rotation runbook is triggered, and an incident is created in PagerDuty. Rollback Procedures Every deployment records an immutable Helm release history in the cluster. Argo CD maintains the complete sync history for each application. A rollback in Argo CD reverts to the previous stable revision within approximately 60 seconds and is the preferred remediation for any regression introduced by a new deployment. Database migrations are managed by golang-migrate. Migrations must be backward compatible with the previous application revision. This means that before removing a column, the application must stop reading from it; before renaming a column, the application must support both names. A migration that is not backward compatible must be staged across multiple releases with explicit coordination between the migration author and the on-call team. If a rollback cannot resolve an incident because the migration is already applied and the previous revision does not support the new schema, the incident commander convenes the relevant service owners to write a forward-fix migration and an application patch. This scenario is considered a process failure and is included in the postmortem. On-Call Operations Each service domain has a named primary and secondary on-call engineer in PagerDuty. Rotations are weekly, starting Monday at 09:00 Pacific. Engineers must acknowledge a PagerDuty alert within 5 minutes. Failure to acknowledge triggers escalation to the secondary. Failure of the secondary to acknowledge within an additional 5 minutes escalates to the on-call engineering manager. Severity 1: customer-impacting service degradation or outage. Requires an incident commander, a dedicated Slack incident channel, and a live status page update within 15 minutes. Resolution is the only priority. Severity 2: degraded service that is not causing direct customer impact but poses a risk of escalation. Addressed during business hours unless the risk assessment changes. Severity 3: non-customer-impacting internal alerts, performance regressions, or elevated error rates within acceptable bounds. Tracked in Jira and addressed in the next sprint. On-call engineers are compensated with a $200 per week on-call stipend regardless of whether any incidents occur. Incidents that require significant out-of-hours work — defined as more than two hours outside of normal working hours in a single week — are compensated with one additional day of PTO. Incident Management and Postmortems All severity 1 and severity 2 incidents require a blameless postmortem document. The postmortem must be published in the internal wiki within five business days of incident resolution. The template requires: a concise incident summary, an impact statement with quantified customer and business effect, a detailed timeline, a root cause analysis, a list of contributing factors, and a set of action items with named owners and target dates.
